rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE ILSE LEMKE TRUST DATED JUNE 17, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (part 2 of 3) The Court finds that Petitioner's effort to place Respondent's mental capacity in controversy amounts to an unsubstantiated allegation. (See Schlagenhauf v. Holder, supra, 379 US at pp. 119-120 [defendant's mere allegation that plaintiff was unfit to drive bus insufficient to support medical examination.].) Schlagenhauf (applying federal law analog to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2032.320) teaches that the "'good cause' and 'in controversy' requirements of Rule 35 make it very apparent that sweeping examinations of a party who has not affirmatively put into issue his own mental or physical condition are not to be automatically ordered merely because the person has been involved in an accident." (Id. at p. 121.) Ms. Lemke did not file litigation putting her capacity at issue, and her objection to an information and belief pleading challenging her capacity does not amount to affirmatively putting her capacity "in controversy" for purposes of this motion. Even if the capacity issue is in controversy, good cause for the examination has not been shown. "[C]ourts must balance the right of civil litigants to discover relevant facts against the privacy interests of persons subject to discovery." (Vinson v. Superior Court, supra, 43 Cal.3d 833 at p. 842.) To protect the examinee's privacy interests from unnecessary intrusion, a mental examination may be ordered only upon a showing of good cause. (Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2032.320 (a); Carpenter v. Superior Court (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 249, 259.) To establish good cause, the movant must provide specific facts justifying discovery and show the inquiry is relevant to the subject matter of the case or reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (Vinson v. Superior Court, supra, 43 Cal.3d at p. 840.) Petitioner's attempt to show good cause is limited; she calls into question the credibility of a third-party witness (Dr. Dralle) and a letter he authored finding Respondent has the capacity to amend her trust, and she concedes that her attorney lacks the requisite credentials to conduct a mental examination of Respondent herself, in the context of a deposition. (Memorandum of Points and Authorities in Support of Petitioner Anna Kauffman's Motion to Compel Independent Medical Examination ("MPA") at p. 5:23-p. 6:10.) This showing is exceedingly thin. Asking a 90-year-old woman to attend a 2 or 3 hour examination with testing and questions from a stranger must be justified by good cause. Petitioner has not provided specific facts justifying the intrusion into Ms. Lemke's privacy and dignity. Finally, there were significant defects in the request for an examination. If a party desires to obtain discovery by mental examination, the party shall obtain leave of court, and the motion for mental examination shall specify the time, place, manner, conditions, scope, and nature of the examination, as well as the identity and the specialty, if any, of the person or persons who will perform the examination. Cal. Code of Civil Proc. secs. 2032.310(a) and (b). Similarly, an order granting a mental examination shall specify the person or persons who may perform the examination as well as the time, place, manner, diagnostic tests and procedures, conditions, scope, and nature of the examination. Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2032.320 (d). The order must specify the diagnostic tests and procedures to be employed-fully and in detail-by listing each of them by name. (Carpenter v. Superior Court, supra, 141 Cal.App.4th at p. 260.) As such, in order for the court to meet the specificity requirement of Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2032.320, the movant must first identify-by name-the potential tests and procedures to be employed in its moving papers.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE ILSE LEMKE TRUST DATED JUNE 17, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: (part 3 of 3) Petitioner's moving papers describe the nature of the mental examination as consisting of "questions, tests, an interview, and a collateral data review related to neurocognitive status and competency as it relates to Ms. Lemke's capacity to appreciate and effectively manage her property and financial affairs or resist undue influence." (MPA, at p. 7:1-4.) Petitioner's Reply to Ilse Lemke's Opposition to Petitioner Anna Kauffman's Motion to Compel Independent Medical Examination identifies eighteen diagnostic tests and procedures to be employed. As described in Carpenter v. Superior Court, supra, 141 Cal.App.4th at p. 261, mental examinations pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2032.320 involve a heightened risk of undue mental invasion because the examinee may not be accompanied by her attorney or other representative who can protect their interests. However, when the court identifies the permissible diagnostic tests and procedures in its order, by name, it confirms that the court has weighed the risks of unwarranted intrusion upon the examinee against the movant's need for the mental examination. (Ibid.) Because Petitioner identified eighteen diagnostic tests and procedures in her reply brief instead of in her initial papers, Respondent was not afforded a meaningful opportunity to apprise the Court concerning those diagnostic tests and procedures. Without hearing Respondent's position as to each of those eighteen diagnostic tests and procedures, the Court cannot adequately weigh the risks of unwarranted intrusion upon the Respondent. The prevailing party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE ILSE LEMKE TRUST DATED JUNE 17, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination
rulings.Rulings: (part 1 of 3) This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the Court is Petitioner Anna Kauffman's Motion to Compel Independent Medical Examination (hereafter the "Motion"). The Motion is DENIED for the reasons discussed below. Respondent Ilse Lemke's Request for Judicial Notice of documents filed with San Francisco Superior Court in Case Number PTR-23-306425, is GRANTED, pursuant to Cal. Evid. Code secs. 451, 452, and 453. As a preliminary matter, the Court cannot and does not consider any information or documents protected by the mediation privilege in making its ruling. (See Cal. Evid. Code sec. 1119.) Any party may obtain discovery by means of a mental examination of their adversary in any action in which the mental condition of that party is in controversy. Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2032.020(a). Although a petitioner's unsubstantiated allegation cannot put the mental state of their adversary in controversy, the respondent can place her mental condition in controversy by asserting an affirmative defense that relies on her mental condition. (Vinson v. Superior Court (1987) 43 Cal.3d 833, 839; Schalfenhauf v. Holder (1964) 379 U.S. 104, 119.) Here, it is not clear to the Court that Petitioner's moving papers adequately establish that Respondent's mental capacity is in controversy. Petitioner filed an amended petition in November of 2023 asserting that Ilse Lemke lacked capacity to amend her trust. The amended petition alleges that, based on conversations Petitioner had with her, "Ms. Lemke seems confused about what is going on with the Trust and her home, consistent with the diagnosis of dementia she received nearly a year ago." (Am. Pet. at par. 1.) The other allegations about lack of capacity focused on Ilse's desire to sell her home in Marin County and thereby incur capital gains tax liability and transaction costs. (Ibid.) Ms. Lemke objected to the amended petition, and noted that the trust here had a provision for determining disability in Section 2: "A Trustor shall be deemed disabled during any period when, in the opinion of two licensed physicians, the Trustor is incapacitated or disabled because of illness, age or any other cause which results in the Trustor's inability to effectively manage her property or financial affairs. Trustor shall also be deemed to be disabled for purposes of this agreement upon a determination of a court of competent jurisdiction that she is incompetent, incapacitated or otherwise legally unable to effectively manage her property or financial affairs." (Trustor's First Amended Objection at pp. 7-8.) Ms. Lemke noted that no such physician letters had been submitted, and that she was entitled to the presumption of capacity found in Probate Code section 810 et. seq. (Id. at p. 9.) She further alleged that the effort to find her incompetent was brought in bad faith, and pointed out that the allegations of incapacity were alleged "on information and belief." (Id. at pp. 6-7, quoting amended petition at Par. 15.)
